entries.definition: A reduction or pause in rent, usually because the space is unusable (for example, after damage).
Money the tenant owes beyond base rent — often taxes, insurance, or operating costs.
A private dispute process where a neutral third party decides the outcome instead of a court.
Transferring the entire lease to a new tenant, who then takes over the rights and obligations.
Lawyer costs that one party may have to pay the other if there is a dispute, depending on the lease terms.
A clause that automatically extends the lease for another term unless the tenant gives notice to cancel by a deadline.
The fixed monthly rent before extras like CAM, taxes, or utilities are added.
Common Area Maintenance — the tenant's share of upkeep for shared spaces like lobbies, parking lots, and hallways.
Charges for the upkeep of shared spaces in a building (often abbreviated CAM).
The date the lease officially starts and rent obligations begin.
A benefit the landlord gives the tenant — such as free rent for the first month or a build-out allowance.
When a government takes property for public use; the lease usually says what happens to rent and the term in that case.
A condition that must be met before a part of the lease takes effect.
Failing to meet a lease obligation, such as not paying rent on time or breaking a rule.
The government's power to take private property for public use, usually with compensation.
A scheduled increase in rent — for example, a fixed percentage each year or tied to an index.
A signed statement confirming basic facts about the lease (rent, term, defaults), often required when the building is sold or refinanced.
The legal process a landlord uses to remove a tenant who has breached the lease.
A clause excusing performance when extraordinary events (storms, war, pandemics) make it impossible.
A person or company that promises to pay or perform if the tenant does not.
When a tenant stays in the space after the lease ends; rent during a holdover is often higher.
To agree to cover another party's losses or legal costs that arise from specified events.
Each tenant or guarantor can be held responsible for the entire obligation, not just their share.
A clause where the parties give up the right to a jury trial for disputes about the lease.
The owner or owner's agent who leases the space to the tenant.
An extra charge for paying rent after the due date or grace period.
The length of time the lease runs, from the commencement date to the expiration date.
Another word for tenant — the person renting the space.
Another word for landlord — the person renting out the space.
A bank's promise to pay the landlord up to a set amount if the tenant defaults; sometimes used instead of a cash deposit.
How far in advance one party must tell the other about an action, such as ending or renewing the lease.
Day-to-day costs of running the building (utilities, taxes, repairs) that may be passed through to tenants.
A right (but not an obligation) for the tenant to extend the lease for an additional term.
The activities the tenant is allowed to conduct in the space; other uses are typically prohibited.
A promise by an individual to be personally responsible for the tenant's obligations under the lease.
The specific space being leased, as described in the lease.
The tenant's right to use the premises without unreasonable interference from the landlord.
Normal aging of the space from ordinary use; tenants usually aren't charged to repair it.
An additional period the lease can be extended for, if the tenant exercises a renewal option.
A temporary reduction or suspension of rent, often after damage or as a concession.
A scheduled rent increase during the term — fixed dollar, fixed percent, or tied to an index.
A right to match a third party's offer (for example, to lease more space or buy the property).
Money the tenant pays up front that the landlord can use against unpaid rent or damage at lease end.
Renting all or part of the premises to someone else while the original tenant remains responsible to the landlord.
Returning the premises to the landlord at the end of the lease, in the condition the lease requires.
The person or company that rents and occupies the premises under the lease.
Build-out work done to customize the space for the tenant, sometimes paid for by the landlord up to an allowance.
The lease section that defines what the tenant can and cannot do in the space.
Giving up a legal right; courts may enforce a written waiver if it is clear and specific.
A landlord's implied promise (mostly in residential leases) that the space is fit to live in.
